title.emphasis: Part I: The Spectrum of IP Acquisition Contracts

section.para: The acquisition of IP rights exists on a spectrum, ranging from complete transfer of ownership to limited permissions for use.

title.emphasis: A. Full Assignment and the Work Made for Hire Doctrine

section.para: The most complete transfer of IP occurs through an outright assignment (purchase) or via the "Work Made for Hire" doctrine under U.S. copyright law. In an employment relationship, IP created within the scope of employment automatically belongs to the employer from inception. This model offers the acquirer maximum control but carries the full burden of employment responsibilities.

title.emphasis: B. Licensing and Leasing (Exclusive vs. Non-Exclusive)

section.para: Licensing allows the creator to retain ownership while granting specific rights to the licensee. This can be exclusive or non-exclusive. The standard model for "free" digital platforms (Meta, X, Google) relies on users granting a "perpetual, worldwide, non-exclusive, royalty-free, transferable, and sublicensable license" to their content in exchange for access to the service. This model is increasingly vulnerable to re-characterization as a contract for services for remuneration.

title.emphasis: C. Unilateral Contracts and the "Service Nexus"

section.para: Platforms often use unilateral contracts, such as Vulnerability Reward Programs (VRPs), offering a reward for the performance of a specific act (e.g., finding a bug). While framed as the purchase of a result, California law holds that such offers become irrevocable once the offeree begins substantial performance (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 45). This creates a "Service Nexus," linking the payment obligation directly to the ongoing labor, making the relationship resemble a service agreement.

An alternative model is the Option Agreement, which involves the present sale of an exclusive right (the option) to purchase a future asset. The buyer pays an option fee for the creator's promise that if they create the work, they will sell it exclusively to the buyer. This model attempts to sever the link between payment and labor, framing the transaction as the purchase of a right, not a service. However, its viability depends heavily on the independent business status of the creator.
